hierarchy: ALWAYS use 'role' tokens in components — never use 'primitive' tokens directly in UI.
ALWAYS use 'semantic' aliases in components — never use raw scale values like radius.scale.8.
ALWAYS use 'gap' aliases for component spacing — never reference space.12 directly in a component.
Primitive tokens exist ONLY to be referenced by role/semantic tokens.
color: text.* tokens are ONLY for text nodes. Never apply them to borders or backgrounds.
border.* tokens are ONLY for strokes and outlines. Never apply them to fills.
background.* tokens are ONLY for full-area fills (page bg, input bg, card bg).
surface.* tokens are ONLY for elevated containers: cards, sheets, tooltips, dropdowns.
icon.* tokens are ONLY for icon fills. Never use text.* on icons.
overlay.* tokens are ONLY for semi-transparent layers: modal backdrops, frosted glass.
Never mix a 'subtle' surface with a 'bold' text color — they are not designed to contrast together.
Never use palette.brand.primary.* or any palette.* directly in components.
typography: body/* is for reading text: paragraphs, descriptions, helper text.
label/* is for UI labels: form labels, tab labels, menu items, list item titles.
title/* is for in-page section headers or card headers — NOT page-level headings.
heading/* is for page-level headings (h1–h3). Use display/* for hero/marketing headings.
button/* is ONLY for button text. Never use it for non-interactive text.
code/* is ONLY for monospace code blocks, inline code, and technical strings.
overline/* is for small ALL-CAPS labels above a title — use sparingly.
Never mix body and label at the same hierarchy level. Body is for reading, label is for UI structure.
spacing: gap/* is for space BETWEEN sibling elements (flexbox gap, grid gap).
space/* is for padding INSIDE a component (padding-top, padding-left, etc.).
Never use gap tokens as padding or space tokens as gap.
focus: ALWAYS apply focus tokens to every interactive element: buttons, inputs, links, checkboxes, selects.
Use focus.light for light mode surfaces. Use focus.dark for dark mode or brand-colored surfaces.
The focus ring must ALWAYS be visible — never suppress outline without replacing it with focus tokens.
ring.inner-color creates a 1px white gap between the element and the outer ring — always keep both.
motion: ALWAYS check motion.reduced.enabled — if true, multiply duration by motion.reduced.duration-multiplier (0 = no animation).
Use duration.fast for micro-interactions (toggles, checkboxes, badges).
Use duration.moderate for standard transitions (hover, focus, dropdown open/close).
Use duration.slow for large layout changes (modals, panels, toasts entering/leaving).
Use duration.extra-slow only for complex multi-step animations or page transitions.
ALWAYS pair a duration with an easing — never use a duration alone.
Use easing.enter for elements appearing. Use easing.exit for elements disappearing. Use easing.standard for elements that stay on screen.
layer: Never set z-index to an arbitrary number — always use a layer.z-index token.
Stacking order: base → raised → sticky → dropdown → overlay → modal → popover → toast → tooltip.
A tooltip (900) always appears above a modal (600). A modal (600) always appears above a dropdown (400).
elevation: Elevation primitives (blur/position) are building blocks — do NOT use them directly in CSS.
Compose them into shadow values using: box-shadow: 0px {position}px {blur}px 0px {color.role.overlay.black}.
Flat surfaces (canvas, subtlest): no shadow. Raised surfaces (cards): shadow with blur.4. Floating (dropdowns): blur.8. Overlays (modals): blur.16.

border.$description: Border width tokens. Use 'width.*' for standard borders. Use 'focus.width' exclusively for focus rings — this ensures focus rings are always 2px regardless of changes to standard border widths.

$usage.width.0: Borderless elements — remove border without removing border-radius.
$usage.width.1: Default border for inputs, cards, dividers, and all standard UI components.
$usage.width.2: Emphasis borders — selected states, active tabs, featured cards.
$usage.width.4: Heavy accent borders — use very sparingly, e.g. left-border accents on alert banners.
$usage.focus.width: ONLY for focus ring width. Always use this token, never hardcode 2px.

$antipatterns: Do NOT use width.2 for regular card borders — use width.1.
Do NOT use width.4 for focus rings — use focus.width.
Do NOT use arbitrary px values for borders — always use a width token.

width.$description: Focus ring border width. Used exclusively by the focus collection.

primitive.$description: RAW COLOR PALETTE. These are the source values for all semantic tokens. DO NOT use these directly in components — always use color.role tokens instead. The only valid consumers of primitive tokens are other tokens.
primitive.$structure: Each color family has 12 stops numbered 100–1200. Lower numbers = lighter (100 is near-white). Higher numbers = darker (1200 is near-black). In Dark mode, the scale inverts — primitive.100 in Dark ≈ primitive.1100 in Light.

$families.brand.primary: Evergreen green — the primary brand color. Used for CTAs, selected states, and brand accents.
$families.brand.secondary: Indigo purple — the secondary brand color. Used for secondary actions and AI/premium features.
$families.brand.accent: Lime yellow-green — accent color. Use very sparingly for highlights and badges only.
$families.neutral: Gray-green neutral scale. Used for all text, borders, surfaces, and backgrounds.
$families.alpha: Semi-transparent neutrals at 60% opacity. Used for overlays and frosted glass effects.

dark.$description: Dark mode palette. Same token names as light, values inverted. The scale is mirrored: dark.100 ≈ light.1100, dark.900 ≈ light.300. Role tokens resolve against this palette automatically in dark mode.

role.$description: SEMANTIC COLOR TOKENS. Use these in all components. Values are aliases to color.primitive — they automatically resolve to the correct value in Light or Dark mode.

text.$description: Text colors. Apply ONLY to text nodes (CSS 'color' property). Never use for borders or backgrounds.

$usage.primary: Main body text, headings, labels. Highest contrast. Example: paragraph text, page titles.
$usage.secondary: Supporting text, descriptions, less prominent labels. Example: card subtitles, helper text.
$usage.tertiary: Placeholder text, timestamps, metadata. Example: 'Posted 3 days ago', input placeholders.
$usage.disabled: Text on disabled interactive elements. Example: a disabled button label.
$usage.inverse: Text on dark/filled surfaces. Example: text on a raised (dark) surface or filled badge.
$usage.selected: Text in selected/active states. Example: active tab label, checked item text.
$usage.evergreen: Brand-colored text. Example: a highlighted stat, a branded heading accent.
$usage.indigo: Secondary brand text. Example: AI-feature labels, premium feature indicators.
$usage.danger: Error and destructive action text. Example: field validation error message.
$usage.info: Informational text. Example: an info banner message.
$usage.warning: Warning text. Example: 'Your session expires in 5 minutes'.

$antipatterns: Do NOT use text.disabled on non-interactive elements — use text.tertiary instead.
Do NOT use text.inverse on light backgrounds — only use on dark/filled surfaces.
Do NOT use text.primary on colored badge backgrounds — use text.inverse or the matching semantic text color.

link.$description: Link colors. Apply to anchor text and interactive inline text only.

$usage.evergreen: Brand-colored link — use on pages where blue would conflict with the brand green context.
$usage.indigo: Secondary brand link — for AI/premium feature links.

icon.$description: Icon fill colors. Apply ONLY to icon SVG fills. Never apply to text or borders.

$usage.primary: Default icon. Example: nav icons, action icons, leading icons in inputs.
$usage.secondary: Subtle icon, less prominent. Example: trailing icons, decorative icons.
$usage.disabled: Icon on a disabled element. Example: icon inside a disabled button.
$usage.inverse: Icon on a dark/filled surface. Example: icon on a raised card.
$usage.selected: Icon on selected/active state. Example: active nav item icon.
$usage.evergreen: Brand-colored icon. Example: branded feature icon.
$usage.indigo: Secondary brand icon. Example: AI feature icon.
$usage.danger: Error/destructive icon. Example: delete icon, error state icon.
$usage.info: Informational icon. Example: info tooltip trigger icon.
$usage.success: Success icon. Example: checkmark in a success banner.
$usage.warning: Warning icon. Example: alert triangle in a warning banner.
$usage.interactive.hovered: Icon color when the parent interactive element is hovered.
$usage.interactive.pressed: Icon color when the parent interactive element is active/pressed.

border.$description: Border/stroke colors. Apply ONLY to CSS border, outline, or box-shadow ring properties — never to fills.

$usage.default: Standard border. Example: input field border, card outline, divider.
$usage.subtle: Very light border, barely visible. Example: table row separator, section divider on white bg.
$usage.strong: High-contrast border. Example: focused input in a non-focus-ring context, emphasized separator.
$usage.disabled: Border on disabled elements. Example: disabled input outline.
$usage.inverse: Border on a dark surface. Example: outline on a raised card.
$usage.focus: Focus indicator border. Prefer using the full focus collection instead.
$usage.selected: Border of a selected item. Example: selected card outline, active tab underline.
$usage.readonly: Border of a readonly input. Lighter than default to signal non-editability.
$usage.evergreen: Brand-colored border. Example: selected evergreen badge outline.
$usage.indigo: Secondary brand border. Example: indigo tag outline.
$usage.danger: Error border. Example: invalid input field border.
$usage.info: Info border. Example: info banner left-accent border.
$usage.success: Success border. Example: valid input field border.
$usage.warning: Warning border. Example: warning banner left-accent border.
$usage.interactive.hovered: Border when hovering an interactive element.
$usage.interactive.pressed: Border when pressing an interactive element.

$pairings.border.default + surface.subtlest: Standard card on a light background.
$pairings.border.danger + surface.danger.subtle: Error state input or form field.
$pairings.border.focus + focus ring: Focused input — use the full focus collection for correct double-ring effect.

background.$description: Full-area background fills. Use for the outermost fill of a component or page region — not for contained surfaces like cards or sheets (use surface.* for those).

$usage.canvas: Page/app background — the base layer everything sits on. Example: <body> or <main> background.
$usage.interactive.hovered: Hover state fill for interactive list items, menu items, table rows. Example: row highlight on hover.
$usage.interactive.pressed: Active/click state fill. Example: pressed state of a list item.
$usage.disabled: Fill for a disabled interactive element's background. Example: disabled input field bg.
$usage.selected: Fill for a selected item's background. Example: selected row in a data table, active menu item.
$usage.readonly: Fill for a readonly input field. Slightly off-white to signal non-editability.
$usage.loading: Semi-transparent overlay on a loading element. Sits on top of content.
$usage.alpha: Semi-transparent background for temporary UI like tooltips or popovers.

$antipatterns: Do NOT use background.canvas for a card — use surface.subtlest or surface.raised.
Do NOT use background.selected for a badge or chip — use surface.evergreen.subtle instead.
Do NOT use background.interactive.hovered as a persistent state — only apply on :hover.

surface.$description: Surface fills for elevated or contained UI containers. Use for cards, sheets, dropdowns, tooltips, sidebars — NOT for full-page backgrounds (use background.canvas) and NOT for interactive row highlights (use background.interactive).

$usage.subtlest: Slightly off-white surface, barely distinguishable from canvas. Example: sidebar background, table header, secondary card on a white page.
$usage.subtle: Light gray surface, clearly distinct from canvas. Example: code block background, inactive tab, tag background.
$usage.raised: Dark surface — inverts text contrast. Example: tooltip background, dark dropdown, dark card. Pair with text.inverse.
$usage.evergreen.subtle: Very light green surface. Example: 'success' badge bg, selected item highlight, empty state illustration bg.
$usage.evergreen.bold: Dark green surface. Example: primary CTA button fill, 'active' status badge.
$usage.indigo.subtle: Very light purple surface. Example: AI feature badge bg, premium indicator tag.
$usage.indigo.bold: Dark purple surface. Example: AI feature filled button, premium badge.
$usage.danger.subtle: Very light red. Example: error alert banner bg, invalid field highlight.
$usage.danger.bold: Dark red. Example: destructive action filled button, critical error badge.
$usage.info.subtle: Very light blue. Example: info alert banner bg, informational note.
$usage.info.bold: Dark blue. Example: info filled badge.
$usage.success.subtle: Very light green (different from evergreen — for status only). Example: success alert banner bg.
$usage.success.bold: Dark green. Example: 'Completed' status badge fill.
$usage.warning.subtle: Very light yellow. Example: warning alert banner bg.
$usage.warning.bold: Dark yellow. Example: 'Attention required' badge fill.

$pairings.surface.evergreen.subtle + text.evergree: Selected state row or evergreen badge.

$antipatterns: Do NOT pair surface.subtle (gray) with text.inverse — insufficient contrast.
Do NOT use surface.raised without text.inverse — dark surface needs light text.
Do NOT use surface.danger.bold with text.danger — bold surface needs text.inverse.
Do NOT use surface.subtlest as a hover state — use background.interactive.hovered.

overlay.$description: Semi-transparent overlay layers. Apply as a fill on top of content — not as a component background.

$usage.white: Frosted glass overlay on light surfaces. Example: loading skeleton shimmer, drag-over highlight.
$usage.black: Dark backdrop behind modals and drawers. Example: modal scrim, side sheet backdrop.

elevation.$description: Primitive elevation components — use these to COMPOSE shadow values, not directly. A shadow is: box-shadow: 0px {position}px {blur}px 0px {color.role.overlay.black}.

$shadow-recipes.sm: Slightly raised (input focus, hovered card): position.1 + blur.2
$shadow-recipes.md: Raised card or popover: position.2 + blur.4
$shadow-recipes.lg: Dropdown or floating panel: position.4 + blur.8
$shadow-recipes.xl: Modal or full overlay: position.4 + blur.16

0.$description: No blur — sharp shadow or no shadow

focus.$description: Focus ring system. ALWAYS apply to every interactive element: buttons, inputs, links, checkboxes, selects, radio buttons, sliders. The ring has two layers: an inner ring (creates a gap between the element and outer ring) and an outer ring (the visible green indicator). This double-ring pattern ensures visibility on both light and dark backgrounds.

$implementation.note: The box-shadow creates the inner gap ring. The outline creates the outer green ring. Both are needed.

$antipatterns: Do NOT use only one ring — the double-ring system is required for WCAG 2.1 AA compliance.
Do NOT suppress the focus ring without a replacement — this breaks keyboard navigation.
Do NOT use focus.light on a dark background — use focus.dark.
Do NOT hardcode ring colors — always reference these tokens.

light.$description: Focus ring for light mode and light-colored surfaces.

color.$description: Alias for the outer ring color — points to color.role.border.focus.

on-brand.$description: Ring color when the focused element is on a brand/dark surface.

offset.$description: Gap between the element edge and the outer ring.

inner-color.$description: Inner gap ring color — white in light mode to create separation.

contrast-color.$description: High-contrast ring for WCAG on complex backgrounds.

inner-width.$description: Width of the inner gap ring (box-shadow).

outer-width.$description: Width of the outer indicator ring (outline).

dark.$description: Focus ring for dark mode. The inner ring becomes dark and the outer ring becomes a lighter green for contrast.

inner-color.$description: Dark inner ring — matches dark bg to create gap.

outer-color.$description: Lighter green #3db856 — more visible on dark surfaces.

layer.$description: Z-index stacking scale. ALWAYS use these tokens — never hardcode z-index values. The scale is intentionally non-linear to allow room for unforeseen intermediate layers without renumbering.
layer.$stacking-order: base(0) → raised(10) → sticky(100) → dropdown(400) → overlay(500) → modal(600) → popover(700) → toast(800) → tooltip(900)

$usage.base: Default document flow — no stacking context needed.
$usage.raised: Slightly lifted elements: sticky table headers within a scroll area, fixed sidebar items.
$usage.sticky: Sticky headers and navigation bars that scroll with the page then fix.
$usage.dropdown: Dropdown menus, select option lists, autocomplete panels.
$usage.overlay: Full-screen semi-transparent backdrops behind modals and drawers.
$usage.popover: Popovers and floating panels that appear above modals (e.g. a date-picker inside a modal).
$usage.toast: Toast notifications and snackbars — must appear above everything except tooltips.

$antipatterns: Do NOT place a dropdown (400) above a modal (600) — it will be hidden.
Do NOT use z-index: 9999 or similar magic numbers — use layer.z-index.tooltip (900) instead.
Do NOT apply layer tokens to non-positioned elements — z-index only works with position: relative/absolute/fixed/sticky.

layout.$description: Responsive layout system. Defines the grid structure across breakpoints. All layouts use a 12-column grid at desktop, 8-column at tablet, 4-column at mobile.

$grid-system.structure: margin | gutter | column | gutter | ... | column | gutter | margin

$usage.breakpoint.mobile: Use as min-width: 360px in media queries. Below this, design for the smallest viewport.
$usage.breakpoint.tablet: Use as min-width: 1024px. Switch from 4-column to 8-column grid.
$usage.breakpoint.computer: Use as min-width: 1440px. Switch from 8-column to 12-column grid.
$usage.columns.*: Number of columns at each breakpoint — use to calculate column spans.
$usage.gutter.*: Space BETWEEN columns — use as column-gap in CSS grid.
$usage.margin.*: Outer padding from screen edge to first/last column — use as padding-inline on the container.

$antipatterns: Do NOT use layout tokens for component-level spacing — use spacing.gap or spacing.space.
Do NOT skip breakpoints — always design and test at mobile (360), tablet (1024), and desktop (1440).
Do NOT use margin.* as component padding — margins only apply to the outermost page container.

mobile.$description: Space between columns. Use as column-gap.
Outer margin from screen edge. Use as padding-inline on the page container.

motion.$description: Animation system. Every animated transition must use a duration + easing pair from this collection. ALWAYS check motion.reduced — if reduced-motion is enabled, multiply duration by motion.reduced.duration-multiplier (which is 0, meaning no animation).
motion.$reduced-motion: if (prefersReducedMotion) { duration = duration * motion.reduced.duration-multiplier; } // result is 0ms = instant

$usage.duration.none: Instant state change — no animation. Example: toggling visibility, programmatic scroll.
$usage.duration.fast: 120ms — micro-interactions. Example: checkbox check, toggle switch, badge count update.
$usage.duration.moderate: 200ms — standard transitions. Example: hover state, focus ring, dropdown open, tooltip appear.
$usage.duration.slow: 320ms — significant layout changes. Example: modal enter/exit, side panel slide, toast appear.
$usage.duration.extra-slow: 480ms — complex sequences. Example: page transition, onboarding animation, multi-step reveal.
$usage.easing.standard: cubic-bezier(0.2,0,0,1) — use for elements that stay on screen (e.g. color change, size change on hover).
$usage.easing.enter: cubic-bezier(0,0,0,1) — use when elements appear/enter (e.g. modal opening, dropdown appearing).
$usage.easing.exit: cubic-bezier(0.4,0,1,1) — use when elements disappear/exit (e.g. modal closing, toast dismissing).
$usage.easing.emphasized: cubic-bezier(0.2,0,0,1.15) — slight overshoot/spring. Use very sparingly for celebratory moments.
$usage.delay.short: 80ms — slight delay for sequential reveals (e.g. menu items appearing one by one).
$usage.delay.long: 240ms — long delay for dramatic reveals or instructional sequences.

$antipatterns: Do NOT use duration.slow for hover effects — use duration.moderate.
Do NOT use easing.enter for exit animations — use easing.exit.
Do NOT ignore motion.reduced — always check and disable animations when it is enabled.
Do NOT use delay.moderate or delay.long for interactive responses — users perceive delays over 100ms as lag.

enabled.$description: Always true — signals that the system respects prefers-reduced-motion.

duration-multiplier.$description: Multiply any duration by this value when reduced-motion is active. 0 = all animations disabled instantly.

opacity.$description: Opacity scale. 'primitive' is the raw numeric scale (0–100, not 0–1). 'state' maps semantic states to opacity values. Always use state tokens in components rather than raw primitive values.
opacity.$note: Values are 0–100, not 0–1. Divide by 100 when writing CSS: opacity.state.disabled = 40 → CSS opacity: 0.4

$usage.state.disabled: 40% — visually indicates an element is non-interactive. Apply to the entire disabled element.
$usage.state.ghost: 10% — nearly invisible placeholder state. Example: ghost button hover, skeleton loading pulse.
$usage.state.overlay: 60% — semi-transparent backdrop. Example: modal scrim (use with color.role.overlay.black).
$usage.state.loading: 60% — loading state overlay on content. Example: table row being updated.
$usage.state.readonly: 100% — full opacity, visually normal but non-editable. Differentiate via border.readonly, not opacity.

$antipatterns: Do NOT use opacity for disabled color — use color.role.text.disabled + color.role.border.disabled instead.
Do NOT apply opacity.state.disabled to the text alone — apply to the entire component.
Do NOT use opacity values not in the primitive scale — stick to 0/10/20/40/60/80/100.

radius.$description: Border-radius tokens. Use 'semantic' aliases in all components — never use raw 'scale' values directly.

$usage.semantic.none: No rounding — sharp corners. Example: dividers, horizontal rules, full-bleed images.
$usage.semantic.xs: 2px — very subtle rounding. Example: tooltip caret, small tags in dense UIs.
$usage.semantic.sm: 4px — slight rounding. Example: input fields, small badges, chips in a list.
$usage.semantic.md: 8px — standard rounding. Example: cards, modals, dropdowns, buttons, alert banners.
$usage.semantic.lg: 12px — generous rounding. Example: large cards, prominent modals, image containers.
$usage.semantic.xl: 16px — heavy rounding. Example: feature highlight cards, hero section containers.
$usage.semantic.pill: 32px — pill shape. Example: status badges, tags, filter chips, toggle switches.
$usage.semantic.full: 999px — fully circular. Example: avatars, FABs, notification dots, icon buttons.

$antipatterns: Do NOT mix semantic.pill with a border — pill + border creates an unexpected visual.
Do NOT use semantic.full on rectangles taller than they are wide — it creates a stadium shape.
Do NOT use arbitrary px values — always use a semantic alias.

spacing.$description: Spacing tokens. 'space' is for internal padding (inside components). 'gap' is for the space between sibling elements. Never swap them.

$usage.space.1: Hairline offset — border adjustments, icon micro-nudges.
$usage.space.2: Extra-tight padding — dense list items, compact badges.
$usage.space.4: Tight padding — icon-only buttons, compact chips.
$usage.space.8: Small padding — standard badge/tag padding, icon button padding.
$usage.space.12: Medium padding — default input padding, small card padding.
$usage.space.16: Standard padding — default card padding, section padding.
$usage.space.24: Large padding — section blocks, large modal padding.
$usage.gap.none: No gap between siblings. Example: button icon + label (they have internal padding).
$usage.gap.xxs: 2px — nearly touching. Example: stacked micro badges.
$usage.gap.xs: 4px — very close. Example: icon + label inside a button, inline tag list.
$usage.gap.sm: 8px — close siblings. Example: form field icon + text, list item elements.
$usage.gap.md: 12px — standard gap. Example: form field + helper text, card element stack.
$usage.gap.lg: 16px — comfortable gap. Example: form fields in a form, nav items, card grid.
$usage.gap.xl: 24px — spacious gap. Example: section elements, page-level component gaps.

$antipatterns: Do NOT use gap.* as padding — gap is for space between siblings only.
Do NOT use space.* as gap in flexbox/grid — use gap.* instead.
Do NOT mix space and gap on the same property — pick the right semantic.
Do NOT use arbitrary px values — always use a space or gap token.

primitive.$description: Raw typography scale values. DO NOT use in components — always use typography.role tokens instead. These exist only to be referenced by role tokens.

body.$description: Primary typeface for all body text, labels, buttons, and UI text.

mono.$description: Monospace typeface for code blocks and technical strings only.

tight.$description: Used on titles, headings, and display text for a more editorial feel.

wide.$description: Used on overlines and small uppercase labels.

regular.$description: Normal text weight — body, descriptions, helper text.

medium.$description: Slightly heavier — labels, nav items, secondary emphasis.

semibold.$description: Strong emphasis — buttons, form labels, titles.

bold.$description: Maximum emphasis — large headings, display text.

role.$description: SEMANTIC TYPOGRAPHY STYLES. Use these in all components. Each role bundles fontFamily + fontSize + lineHeight + fontWeight + letterSpacing.
role.$hierarchy: body (reading) → label (UI structure) → title (section headers) → heading (page headers) → display (hero/marketing)

$usage.body.xs: 12px/16 regular — footnotes, captions, tooltip text, table cell secondary text.
$usage.body.sm: 14px/20 regular — default body text for most UI content: descriptions, helper text, list item text.
$usage.body.md: 16px/20 regular — larger body text: article paragraphs, empty state descriptions.
$usage.body.md-strong: 16px/20 semibold — emphasized body text: inline bold within a paragraph, key terms.
$usage.body.lg: 18px/24 regular — intro paragraphs, large descriptions in hero sections.
$usage.label.xs: 12px/16 medium wide-tracking — overline-style labels, ALL-CAPS micro labels (though use overline.md instead).
$usage.label.sm: 12px/16 medium — very compact UI labels: table column headers, dense form field labels.
$usage.label.md: 14px/20 medium — standard form field labels, nav item text, menu item text, chip text.
$usage.label.lg: 16px/20 semibold — prominent UI labels: tab labels, section labels, prominent list item titles.
$usage.title.sm: 18px/24 semibold tight — card titles, dialog titles, sidebar section headers.
$usage.title.md: 20px/28 semibold tight — page section titles, widget titles, panel headers.
$usage.title.lg: 24px/36 bold tight — primary section headings within a page.
$usage.heading.lg: 30px/36 bold tight — h1-level headings on content pages.
$usage.heading.xl: 36px/56 bold tight — primary page headings on dashboard/app pages.
$usage.display.sm: 36px/56 bold tight — hero headings on landing pages.
$usage.display.md: 48px/56 bold tight — extra-large hero headings, splash screens.
$usage.button.sm: 12px/16 semibold — small button labels (sm button variant).
$usage.button.md: 14px/16 semibold — default button label (md button variant).
$usage.button.lg: 16px/24 semibold — large button label (lg button variant).
$usage.code.sm: 12px/16 mono regular — inline code snippets, small code annotations.
$usage.code.lg: 16px/24 mono regular — large code editors or prominent code displays.
$usage.overline.md: 12px/16 semibold wide-tracking — small labels above a title: 'CATEGORY', 'STEP 1 OF 3', section pre-labels.

$antipatterns: Do NOT use heading.* for card titles — use title.*.
Do NOT use body.* for button labels — use button.*.
Do NOT use label.* for reading paragraphs — use body.*.
Do NOT use display.* inside a card or dialog — it is too large for contained UI.
Do NOT use code.* for non-code text — it signals technical content.
Do NOT mix heading and title at the same visual level — pick one per hierarchy.
